title: Ben Franklin

jokes.text: For your convenience, I've broken it down into three parts: professional resume, athletic and special skills resume, and Dwight Schrute trivia.
jokes.setup: Dwight presenting his job search materials in a professional manner
jokes.punchline: Including 'Dwight Schrute trivia' as a legitimate resume category
jokes.explanation: Perfect Dwight character moment - his complete lack of self-awareness in thinking 'Dwight Schrute trivia' is a professional selling point. Shows his grandiose self-image.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: As you can see, my resume includes all the standard categories: work experience, education, certifications, references. But I've also added a section that I believe sets me apart from other candidates: 'Dwight Schrute Trivia.' Did you know I'm a volunteer sheriff's deputy? Did you know I grow the most beautiful beets in the state? Did you know I have a black belt in several martial arts? These are the kinds of things employers need to know about me.

jokes.text: How would I describe myself? Three words: hardworking, alpha male, jackhammer. Merciless. Insatiable.
jokes.setup: Standard self-description question, promises three words
jokes.punchline: Gives five increasingly inappropriate 'words', including 'jackhammer'
jokes.explanation: Classic Dwight - can't count, escalates inappropriately, and 'jackhammer' as self-description is absurdly specific and sexual without him realizing it.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: I'm going to describe myself in three words.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Hardworking.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Alpha male.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Jackhammer. Merciless. Insatiable.

jokes.text: There's nothing on my horizon except everything. Everything is on my horizon.
jokes.setup: Dwight trying to sound profound about his future
jokes.punchline: Contradictory statement that makes no logical sense
jokes.explanation: Typical Dwight pseudo-wisdom that sounds meaningful but is actually nonsensical. Character-appropriate but not particularly memorable.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: I'm going to live forever, or die trying.

jokes.text: Ooops. Break's over.
jokes.setup: Dwight has been giving this elaborate confessional about his job search
jokes.punchline: Reveals he's supposed to be working at his current job while doing this
jokes.explanation: Good callback to reality - shows Dwight's unprofessionalism and adds cringe element. Economy is excellent but not groundbreaking.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: I've been networking, updating my resume, reaching out to contacts in the beet farming industry...

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Wait. I'm supposed to be working right now, aren't I?

jokes.text: All four parts. Recorded it on my computer. Took me forever.
jokes.setup: Andy is singing and Jim asks if it's him
jokes.punchline: Andy proudly admits to recording himself singing harmony with himself
jokes.explanation: Perfect Andy character moment - his complete lack of embarrassment about something that should be embarrassing. Shows his obliviousness.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Is that you singing?

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Yeah! I recorded myself singing harmony with myself.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Pretty good, right?

jokes.text: I called it myself. Just thought you'd get a kick out of the new ring.
jokes.setup: Andy's phone rings during conversation
jokes.punchline: He admits to calling his own phone just to show off his ringtone
jokes.explanation: Excellent cringe moment - the pathetic lengths Andy goes to for attention. The casualness of his admission makes it funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Yeah, I called my own phone. So what? This ringtone is incredible. I wanted everyone to hear it.

jokes.text: I'd just like to say I'm thrilled to be working directly beneath you. I feel I have a lot to learn from you, even though you are younger and have less experience.
jokes.setup: Andy trying to be deferential to Jim
jokes.punchline: Immediately undermines his deference by pointing out Jim's youth and inexperience
jokes.explanation: Good character work showing Andy's inability to genuinely be humble. The structure of building up then immediately tearing down works well.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Jim, I want you to know that I respect your authority as manager.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: But let's be honest, you're what, 35? You've only been managing for a few years. I've got way more life experience.

jokes.text: Andy and the Tuna.
jokes.setup: Andy proposing a toast to their partnership
jokes.punchline: The awkward nickname pairing that sounds like a bad band name
jokes.explanation: The nickname 'Tuna' for Jim becomes a running gag. The awkward formality of creating a 'duo name' is very Andy.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: To our partnership—we're like a dynamic duo. I'm thinking we call ourselves... 'Bernard and Butz.'

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: That sounds like a 1970s folk duo that nobody asked for.

jokes.text: Andy and the Tuna...
jokes.setup: Andy's enthusiastic toast
jokes.punchline: Jim's reluctant, uncomfortable repetition
jokes.explanation: Jim's deadpan reaction to Andy's enthusiasm. The ellipsis suggests his discomfort and reluctance.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: To friendship! To love! To new beginnings!

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: To... friendship. Love. New beginnings.

jokes.text: I miss Dwight.
jokes.setup: Jim has been dealing with Andy's over-eagerness
jokes.punchline: Admits to missing Dwight, his former antagonist
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy - three words that capture Jim's situation perfectly. Ironic that he misses his nemesis because the replacement is worse.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: You know what? I actually miss Dwight. At least he was honest about being annoying. Andy's annoying AND he's trying to be my friend about it.

jokes.text: Congratulations, universe. You win.
jokes.setup: Jim's frustration with his situation
jokes.punchline: Dramatically surrendering to cosmic forces
jokes.explanation: Great Jim moment - his dry delivery of melodramatic surrender. Relatable and quotable frustration.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: You know what? I give up. The universe wins. I'm just going to accept that nothing I do matters and that I'm cosmically insignificant.

jokes.text: Was- the top salesman. I said, 'was'.
jokes.setup: Michael trying to downplay Dwight's departure
jokes.punchline: Awkwardly correcting his own grammar while revealing his defensiveness
jokes.explanation: Michael's awkward overcompensation shows he knows Dwight leaving is bad but won't admit it. The grammar correction is petty and defensive.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Dwight's leaving is not a big deal. I mean, it's not like he's... I'm not even thinking about it. We're fine. Everything's fine here without him. I mean, without him here. Grammar-wise. The point is, we don't need Dwight. I've managed perfectly well before Dwight, and I'll manage perfectly well after Dwight. Who even is Dwight?

jokes.text: Addition by subtraction. What does that even mean? That is impossible. Yeah, you're right.
jokes.setup: Michael uses corporate buzzword 'addition by subtraction'
jokes.punchline: Jim immediately calls out the logical impossibility, Michael agrees
jokes.explanation: Good setup-punchline with Jim as straight man. Michael's immediate capitulation shows his lack of conviction in his own corporate speak.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: We need to think about addition by subtraction here.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: That's not... that doesn't make mathematical sense.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: You know what, you're right. I have no idea what that means.

jokes.text: Addition by addition.
jokes.setup: Previous 'addition by subtraction' nonsense
jokes.punchline: Michael tries the opposite phrase, which is just redundant
jokes.explanation: Immediate callback that shows Michael doesn't understand why the first phrase was wrong. Simple but effective.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: You know what? I'm gonna try the opposite. Addition by subtraction... subtraction by addition.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: That's just addition.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Exactly.

jokes.text: How was your gay-cation? That's very funny. Yeah, I thought of that like two seconds after you left.
jokes.setup: Michael greeting Oscar back from leave
jokes.punchline: Admits he's proud of a pun he came up with after Oscar left
jokes.explanation: Typical Michael - proud of a mediocre pun and admitting he thought of it too late. Shows his awkwardness around the gay situation.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Oscar, welcome back! I have to tell you, I came up with this amazing pun while you were gone and I've been dying to use it on you.

dialogue.speaker: Oscar
dialogue.line: Oh no.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: I'm really proud of this one. You ready?

dialogue.speaker: Oscar
dialogue.line: Not really.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: That's the spirit! Okay, here it is: I was thinking about you the other day, and I realized... you're like a fine wine. You get better with age. Oscar, get it? Because you're... old.

dialogue.speaker: Oscar
dialogue.line: That's not a pun, Michael.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: It is though. And I'm very proud of it.

jokes.text: TGI Wednesday. Am I right? Yeap. Gonna go home, get my beer on, get my Lost on.
jokes.setup: Andy trying to bond with Jim about end of workday
jokes.punchline: Forced casual talk including 'get my Lost on' phrasing
jokes.explanation: Andy trying too hard to sound casual and cool. 'Get my Lost on' is period-appropriate awkward phrasing that shows his try-hard nature.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: So Jim, what are you doing after work? I'm gonna go home, grab a beer, get my Lost on.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Lost on?

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Yeah, you know, watch Lost. Get my Lost on.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: I don't think that's a thing.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: It's totally a thing. You get your Lost on, I get my Lost on, we all get our Lost on.

jokes.text: I'll take that as a 'Maybe'
jokes.setup: Jim gives noncommittal response to hanging out
jokes.punchline: Andy optimistically reframes rejection as hope
jokes.explanation: Shows Andy's desperate optimism and inability to read social cues. His reframing of obvious disinterest as possibility is painfully hopeful.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Yeah, maybe. We'll see.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Did you hear that? 'We'll see.' That's basically a yes. Jim wants to hang out with me!

jokes.text: Where're you going? Bathroom. Oh, I'm about to go to kitchen. I'll walk with you.
jokes.setup: Normal question about where Jim is going
jokes.punchline: Andy immediately offers to accompany him, showing clingy behavior
jokes.explanation: Perfect Andy stalker behavior - can't let Jim out of his sight even for bathroom breaks. The casual way he offers shows how oblivious he is to boundaries.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: I'm going to grab some coffee.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Oh! Can I come with you?

jokes.text: Things are going pretty good. Getting a lot of face time with the boss.
jokes.setup: Andy's confessional about how work is going
jokes.punchline: He thinks his annoying behavior constitutes positive 'face time'
jokes.explanation: Ironic because we've seen Jim trying to escape Andy. Shows Andy's complete misreading of the situation and his corporate mindset.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I'm really trying to maximize my face time with management. Every interaction is an opportunity to show them I'm engaged and enthusiastic.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Some people might call it annoying, but I call it being proactive. The more they see me, the more they remember me when promotion time comes around.

jokes.text: Because I'm gay. No- no- Certain events have transpired, and I've thought about certain things.
jokes.setup: Angela asking Oscar to join party planning committee
jokes.punchline: Oscar assumes it's because he's gay, Angela's awkward corporate-speak response
jokes.explanation: Great callback to the kiss incident. Angela's corporate euphemisms ('certain events have transpired') are perfectly awkward for her character.

dialogue.speaker: Angela
dialogue.line: Oscar, we'd love to have you join the party planning committee. We think you'd bring a lot to the table.

dialogue.speaker: Oscar
dialogue.line: Is this because I'm gay?

dialogue.speaker: Angela
dialogue.line: What? No! We just think you have a lot of... creative sensibilities and an eye for detail and... well, you know, we value diversity on our committee.

jokes.text: Can I join too? Never.
jokes.setup: Kevin seeing Oscar get invited to party planning
jokes.punchline: Angela's immediate, definitive rejection
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy and timing. Angela's instant, harsh rejection of Kevin shows her standards and Kevin's low status. One word punchline is devastating.

dialogue.speaker: Angela
dialogue.line: Absolutely not.

jokes.text: Oh, that wasn't the night crew. That was Dwight. Really? It was very nice of him.
jokes.setup: Michael wondering who arranged his desk toys nicely
jokes.punchline: Learning it was Dwight, whom he just fired
jokes.explanation: Reveals Dwight's hidden kindness and Michael's obliviousness to losing a loyal employee. Well-earned emotional beat.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Who arranged all my desk toys so nicely?

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: That was Dwight.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Dwight? But I just fired him!

jokes.text: Feel ya, dawg. Yeah, do you? Absolutely. What did I say?
jokes.setup: Andy trying to sound cool with slang
jokes.punchline: Michael tests him and Andy can't repeat what he said
jokes.explanation: Shows Andy's fake persona - using slang he doesn't understand. Michael calling him out is rare moment of Michael being perceptive.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Yo, I'm totally down with the lingo. I'm hip, I'm cool, I'm with it.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Okay, okay. Say 'cromulent.'

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Cromulent.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Now say it again.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Uh... crumbulent?

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: That's not even a word.

jokes.text: He's always up in my bid-ness, which is Ebonics for 'being in my face and annoying the bejesus out of me.'
jokes.setup: Michael complaining about Andy
jokes.punchline: Awkward explanation of 'Ebonics' that's patronizing and incorrect
jokes.explanation: Peak Michael cringe - trying to sound street while being completely tone-deaf. The formal explanation of slang defeats the purpose.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Andy's always trying to use slang he doesn't understand. Like, he'll just randomly say things that make no sense. It's like he's trying to speak... Ebonics or whatever. You know, that's the language where black people talk... differently. With the words all... rearranged and stuff. It's actually a very real dialect, very sophisticated, but when Andy does it, he just sounds like an idiot.

jokes.text: Your gayness does not define you. Your Mexicanness is what defines you, to me, and I think we should celebrate Oscar's Mexicanity.
jokes.setup: Michael trying to be politically correct about Oscar
jokes.punchline: Immediately contradicts himself and makes up 'Mexicanity'
jokes.explanation: Classic Michael - trying to be progressive while being more offensive. 'Mexicanity' is a perfect Michael-ism. The logical contradiction is perfectly executed.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Oscar, you are a valued member of this office, and your Mexican heritage is—I mean, your Mexican-American heritage is something we all respect and admire.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: In fact, I would say you have more Mexicanity than anyone I know.

jokes.text: So, Phyllis? I want you to go find firecrackers and a chihuahua.
jokes.setup: Michael planning Oscar's Mexican-themed party
jokes.punchline: Requesting the most stereotypical Mexican items possible
jokes.explanation: Michael's casual racism reaches absurd heights. The specificity of 'firecrackers and a chihuahua' as party supplies is perfectly Michael.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Okay, for Oscar's party, I'm thinking we go full Mexican. I want sombreros, I want maracas, I want a piñata shaped like... a Mexican.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: Michael, that's—

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: And a taco bar. A really big taco bar. With those little umbrellas in the drinks.

dialogue.speaker: Oscar
dialogue.line: Those are for tropical drinks.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Exactly. We'll make it work.

jokes.text: Why don't you have me riding in on a donkey, into the office, like Pepe? Ah, a burro, of course. If Oscar wants a donkey, let's get him one.
jokes.setup: Oscar sarcastically suggesting riding a donkey
jokes.punchline: Michael takes it seriously and corrects 'donkey' to 'burro'
jokes.explanation: Perfect Michael moment - missing obvious sarcasm and doubling down with 'correction' to burro. Shows his complete obliviousness to how offensive he's being.

dialogue.speaker: Oscar
dialogue.line: Why don't you just ride a donkey?

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: A burro. It's called a burro.

jokes.text: I will literally be standing right here if you need anything at all.
jokes.setup: Andy offering to help Jim who said he doesn't need help
jokes.punchline: Promise to hover nearby anyway, missing the social cue
jokes.explanation: Andy's stalker behavior continues. The 'literally' emphasizes how he completely misses that hovering is unwanted.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Well, I'm going to hover nearby anyway, just in case you change your mind.

jokes.text: I think I could go for some tuna fish right about now. Got my rod here.
jokes.setup: Andy continues to hover near Jim
jokes.punchline: Makes fishing pun about Jim's 'Tuna' nickname while miming fishing
jokes.explanation: Andy's continued stalking with forced pun. The fishing metaphor for pursuing Jim's attention is apt but cringey.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: You know, Jim, I've been thinking... with a nickname like 'Tuna,' you must be great at fishing. I'd love to take you out sometime. We could really reel in some laughs.

jokes.text: Each file is password-protected with a different mythical creature, so...
jokes.setup: Jim explaining why he can't help prank Andy
jokes.punchline: Reveals Dwight's absurd password system
jokes.explanation: Perfect Dwight detail that shows his fantasy obsession and paranoia. Specific enough to be believable for his character and absurd enough to be funny.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: I can't help prank Andy right now. Dwight's got this whole password system that's completely insane.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: It's like, he changes it every day based on what he had for breakfast, and it has to include a number, a symbol, and something about beets.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Last week it was 'Schrute$Farms47!'. This week? 'Beets#Forever1823'.

jokes.text: Who's that sportscaster that bit that lady? Marv something? Andy is like Marv something.
jokes.setup: Jim trying to describe Andy's creepy behavior
jokes.punchline: Compares him to a sportscaster who bit someone but can't remember the name
jokes.explanation: Specific but vague reference that captures Andy's unsettling quality. The inability to remember the name makes it more realistic and funny.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: He's like this sportscaster who bit someone, but I can't remember his name.

jokes.text: Do you want to pull a prank on Andy? Not right now. But ask me again ten years ago.
jokes.setup: Jim asking Ryan to help with prank
jokes.punchline: Ryan's time-travel response showing he's over pranking
jokes.explanation: Excellent structure and originality. Ryan's response is perfectly dismissive while being absurdly specific. Shows his character evolution from temp to corporate.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Ryan, I need your help with a prank.

dialogue.speaker: Ryan
dialogue.line: Jim, I've come from the future to tell you that pranks don't matter. In 200 years, no one will remember Dwight's stapler being in Jell-O.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: So that's a no?

dialogue.speaker: Ryan
dialogue.line: That's a no.

jokes.text: I liked you better as the temp. Me too.
jokes.setup: Following Ryan's dismissive response
jokes.punchline: Ryan agreeing he was better before his promotion
jokes.explanation: Good callback showing Ryan's self-awareness about how his promotion changed him. The agreement is unexpectedly honest.

dialogue.speaker: Ryan
dialogue.line: Yeah, I was better before I got promoted.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: No, I mean before you were born.

jokes.text: Oh sure. We talk all the time. Really? No. Don't- Don't do that. That's not nice.
jokes.setup: Michael asking if anyone talks to Dwight
jokes.punchline: Jim's fake-out followed by immediate contradiction
jokes.explanation: Classic misdirection setup. Michael's scolding shows he's sensitive about Dwight leaving despite trying to act indifferent.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Does anyone actually talk to Dwight?

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: No, not really.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: I talk to him all day.

jokes.text: Dwight had a big personality and I have a big personality, and a lot of times, when two people like that get together, it can be explosive.
jokes.setup: Michael explaining his relationship with Dwight
jokes.punchline: Framing their conflict as two big personalities rather than his poor management
jokes.explanation: Michael's self-serving narrative about the Dwight situation. Shows his inability to take responsibility while complimenting himself.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Dwight and I have a very special relationship. It's not a boss-employee thing. It's more like... two alpha males who are constantly butting heads because we're both so driven and passionate.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: People see the conflict and they think it's about management style, but really it's just two titans in the same space. We're like oil and water. Or fire and ice. Two forces of nature that can't coexist peacefully.

jokes.text: Have you heard of paper? It's gonna be like that, huh? I don't like him, his giant head, or his beady little eyes.
jokes.setup: Dwight's new boss hasn't heard of Dunder Mifflin
jokes.punchline: Dwight's sarcastic response and immediate character assassination
jokes.explanation: Perfect Dwight response - immediately combative and specific in his insults. Shows he's not adapting well to new workplace.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Dunder Mifflin? Never heard of it.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Well, we're only the second-largest paper distributor in the state. But sure, you've never heard of us. That's... that's fine. Clearly you've been too busy not reading industry reports or, I don't know, doing your job.

jokes.text: Would you like to pull a prank on Andy? I'm kind of in the middle of ... yes, please.
jokes.setup: Jim interrupting Pam who seems busy
jokes.punchline: Pam immediately abandoning her excuse to join in
jokes.explanation: Good structure showing Pam's enthusiasm overriding her initial hesitation. Shows their partnership and shared dislike of Andy.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Hey, you busy?

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: Actually, I was just—

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Great, let's go.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: Okay!

jokes.text: Large Tuna, have you seen my cell phone device?
jokes.setup: Andy looking for his phone
jokes.punchline: Calls it 'cell phone device' in awkwardly formal way
jokes.explanation: Andy's formal speech pattern ('cell phone device') shows his trying-too-hard personality. Sets up the prank payoff.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Has anyone seen my phone?

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: If you find my cellular phone device, please let me know immediately.

jokes.text: [Phone ringing in ceiling - Andy looking around confused]
jokes.setup: Previous setup of Jim and Pam pranking Andy with hidden phone
jokes.punchline: Andy hearing his phone ring from impossible location
jokes.explanation: Classic Jim/Pam prank execution. Visual comedy of Andy's confusion as phone rings from ceiling. Well-earned payoff to setup.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Where is that coming from?

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: What's wrong?

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: My phone is ringing but I can't find it.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: Have you checked your desk?

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Of course I checked my desk! It sounds like it's coming from... inside the wall?

jokes.text: You know what? Maybe it's in the ceiling. You know what? Maybe you're in the ceiling!
jokes.setup: Jim 'helpfully' suggesting phone might be in ceiling
jokes.punchline: Andy's nonsensical angry response
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation and quotable line. Andy's illogical comeback shows his frustration boiling over into absurdity. Great economy and timing.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Your phone might be in the ceiling.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Why would my phone be in the ceiling?! That doesn't make any sense, Jim!

jokes.text: Oh my god. That's half-inch drywall. I think we broke his brain.
jokes.setup: Andy has presumably punched through the ceiling looking for his phone
jokes.punchline: Jim and Pam's assessment of the damage, both physical and mental
jokes.explanation: Great callback and escalation of prank consequences. 'I think we broke his brain' is quotable and shows the prank went too far.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: So he punched through the ceiling looking for his phone.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: His phone was in his pocket the whole time.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Yeah. And now there's a hole in the ceiling, and Andy's hand is... well, it's not great.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: He kept yelling, 'Why isn't anyone helping me?' We were all just standing there.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: I think we all learned something today.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: What's that?

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Andy should not be allowed to have a phone.

jokes.text: Sorry I annoyed you with my friendship.
jokes.setup: Andy's overeager attempts to bond with Jim have been rejected
jokes.punchline: Passive-aggressive response that completely misframes the situation
jokes.explanation: Instant classic line. Perfect Andy character moment - passive aggressive, self-pitying, and completely missing the point. Highly quotable and captures his delusional self-image perfectly.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: You know what? Fine. I didn't want to hang out with you anyway. I was just trying to be nice because I thought we could be friends. But clearly you think you're too good for me.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Andy, I literally just said I was busy today.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Right, right. 'Busy.' That's what people say when they don't want to hurt your feelings. It's cool. I get it. You and Pam have your little inside jokes, and I'm just the annoying guy who tries too hard. Well, joke's on you, because I'm going to go have fun without you, and when you see how much fun I'm having, you're going to regret this decision.

jokes.text: You should come back. Please. I don't want to do your laundry anymore.
jokes.setup: Michael asking Dwight to return to work
jokes.punchline: Reveals he's been doing Dwight's laundry, suggesting codependent relationship
jokes.explanation: Perfect reveal of Michael and Dwight's bizarre relationship. Shows how dependent Michael was on Dwight beyond work. Great economy and character insight.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Dwight, you need to come back to work. I can't do this anymore.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Do what?

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Your laundry. I've been doing your laundry.

jokes.text: Do you still have feelings for her? [pause] Yes.
jokes.setup: Jim asking Pam about her feelings during romantic moment
jokes.punchline: The awkward pause before honest admission
jokes.explanation: Important character moment disguised as comedy through awkward timing. The pause and honesty create tension and humor simultaneously.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: So... what are you really feeling right now?

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: I'm terrified.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Of what?

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: That I'm going to wake up and this will all be a dream.

jokes.text: I want them sucking up to me because they genuinely love me.
jokes.setup: Michael reflecting on what he learned from the Andy situation
jokes.punchline: Still wants people sucking up to him, just for 'better' reasons
jokes.explanation: Perfect Michael ending - thinks he's learned a lesson but completely misses the point. Shows he still wants sycophants, just 'authentic' ones.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: I learned a valuable lesson from the Andy situation. I realized that I don't want people sucking up to me anymore. I want them to suck up to me for better reasons.

tmdb_overview: Michael celebrates Phyllis' wedding shower by bringing two adult performers into the office, a stripper and Ben Franklin. Meanwhile, Karen confronts Pam about her past history with Jim.
insight: Ben Franklin impersonator triggers 45 jokes in 45 minutes—pure character-comedy density.
